name: Demonstrator Platform and Scope Sequencing
consequences: Choosing which authentication pathway to prototype first (mobile-only, hybrid mobile-plus-fallback, or browser-first) directly shapes stakeholder credibility and shapes the technical evidence available for phase-three procurement language. A mobile-focused demonstrator on GrapheneOS shows platform-neutral feasibility but requires explaining why average citizens would adopt a non-mainstream OS; a browser-based or hardware-token fallback demonstrator is less dependent on OS choice but may seem too niche for critical public services. The choice will determine which constituencies (privacy advocates, enterprise IT, government continuity planners) regard the project as addressing their core concern.
options: Develop the mobile demonstrator on GrapheneOS first to establish credible technical proof that FIDO2 and OpenID Connect can deliver secure authentication without Apple or Google platform integrity, then expand into browser or hardware-token fallback in phase two as a secondary proof point.

Lead with a browser-based or hardware-token fallback demonstrator that works across all devices and operating systems, treating the mobile-only approach as secondary, in order to ground the fallback narrative in something more universally deployable and less dependent on users switching to privacy-focused Android variants.

Prototype both authentication pathways in parallel with minimal scope per pathway, accepting slower depth of technical validation on each but maximizing the range of regulatory and procurement stakeholders who see at least one demonstrator aligned with their own infrastructure assumptions and constraints.
review: Platform choice determines credibility with different audiences, but the core tension is between technical rigor and breadth of institutional buy-in. A GrapheneOS-focused approach demonstrates platform-neutrality convincingly but risks being dismissed as 'too niche for mainstream public services'; a browser-based fallback is more familiar to IT administrators but may underexplore mobile authentication, which is how most citizens currently access MitID. None of the options adequately address how to handle the demonstrators' legal and liability status—they are intentionally non-production and not MitID-certified, which leaves government stakeholders uncertain whether they can legitimately reference or build upon the work without MitID operator pushback.

name: Coalition Building: Civil Society versus Direct Government Engagement
consequences: The project can prioritize either a grassroots-and-civil-society foundation (NGOs, privacy advocates, researchers) that amplifies external pressure on Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, or direct institutional engagement with government stakeholders and procurement authorities. The civil-society route builds democratic legitimacy and resilience against individual administrative shifts but may be perceived as advocacy rather than technical expertise; the direct-engagement route offers faster institutional traction but creates dependency on a few officials' continued support and leaves the project vulnerable to policy reversals or incumbent pressure. The choice shapes whether the project's power comes from coalition scale and persistence or from embedded institutional relationships.
options: Prioritize direct formal engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and AltID governance structures, scheduling regular technical briefings and procurement consultations to position the project as internal advisory rather than external advocacy, trading breadth of public coalition for institutional credibility.

Build a visible coalition of privacy-focused NGOs, digital-rights organizations, academic institutions, and sympathetic elected officials first, then approach government stakeholders from a position of demonstrated public and institutional support, accepting slower initial government access in exchange for sustained external pressure and political resilience.

Maintain dual-track engagement: cultivate a small core coalition of credible researchers and civil-society leaders in parallel with selective one-on-one government stakeholder meetings, ensuring neither track dominates but using each to reinforce the other's legitimacy.
review: The tension is between institutional access and institutional independence. Direct government engagement risks co-option or quiet shelving if procurement priorities shift; civil-society pressure can sustain the issue but may provoke incumbent pushback framed as 'politicizing technical infrastructure.' The most critical weakness across all options is the absence of a clear escalation pathway if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or the MitID operator formally rejects or delays the platform-neutrality proposal; none of the options specify how to invoke higher-level political pressure, EU-level review, or public transparency mechanisms if bureaucratic resistance proves insurmountable.

name: Timing of EU-Level Standards Engagement
consequences: The project can engage EU-level Digital Identity Wallet and eIDAS working groups early (phase one or early phase two) to position Danish advocacy within broader European interoperability and portability discussions, or defer EU engagement until demonstrators and domestic policy gains create leverage to contribute credible technical input (phase three). Early EU engagement amplifies resilience arguments at a regulatory scale but may dissipate focus and resources before domestic Danish buy-in is secured; late engagement maximizes the project's leverage but risks arriving after key EU design and procurement decisions have already hardened around existing platforms. The timing also affects whether the project shapes EU requirements or merely aligns with them.
options: Initiate EU-level engagement in phase one by presenting the Danish platform-dependency risk as a case study within ongoing European Digital Identity Wallet consultations, positioning Denmark as a model for EU-wide platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements before final specifications are locked.

Defer substantive EU engagement until phase three, after demonstrators are complete and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has formally responded to the domestic proposal, then use the Danish feasibility work and procurement precedent to influence European working groups and contribute to revised Digital Identity Wallet materials.

Maintain a light advisory presence in relevant EU technical working groups throughout the project (starting phase one), without committing major resources, in order to stay informed of EU priorities and timing while preserving flexibility to escalate EU engagement if domestic Danish progress stalls.
review: The timing question masks a deeper institutional reality: EU-level Digital Identity Wallet work is on a fixed timeline driven by eIDAS2 implementation deadlines, and those deadlines may not align with Danish domestic procurement cycles. Engaging too early risks the project's input being shelved or watered down before domestic Danish leverage is built; engaging too late means the project's technical input arrives after key EU architecture decisions are finalized. None of the options adequately account for the possibility that EU working groups may lack appetite for platform-neutrality language if major member states' procurement is already committed to ecosystem-wide Apple or Google solutions.

name: Fallback Access Model: Scope and Inclusivity
consequences: The project must define whether the platform-neutral fallback is intended as a genuine primary access path for all citizens (raising inclusivity, UX, and deployment scope requirements) or as a contingency mechanism for service continuity and edge cases (reducing scope but potentially limiting adoption and political buy-in). A true primary-access fallback requires addressing accessibility, digital-literacy support, and hardware availability, substantially increasing budget and implementation complexity; a contingency-focused fallback can be leaner but may be dismissed by policymakers as a niche feature insufficient to justify reducing iOS/Android dependence. The choice shapes the regulatory framing and determines which constituencies the fallback must satisfy.
options: Design the fallback authentication path explicitly as a universal primary-access mechanism supporting all citizen segments, including low-digital-literacy users and those without smartphones, requiring hardware-token and browser-based pathways that are equally accessible, usable, and discoverable as the mobile authentication, with supporting rollout and service-desk infrastructure planning.

Scope the fallback strictly as a service-continuity and contingency mechanism for critical public-service access during platform outages or policy changes, requiring the fallback to be secure and functional but not necessarily optimized for UX or universal accessibility, positioning it as a resilience safeguard rather than a primary citizen-facing interface.

Develop the fallback as a hybrid: a full-featured contingency path that scales to primary-access capability if deployment capacity becomes available, starting with narrow scope (government employees, high-risk public services) and designed for modular expansion rather than attempting to optimize for all use cases simultaneously.
review: The fallback scope directly constrains the political argument: a universal primary-access fallback is more defensible on resilience grounds but requires far greater implementation investment and faces more complex UX and accessibility validation; a contingency-only fallback is cheaper but is easier for incumbents to characterize as unnecessary complexity. The deepest tension is that resilience framing (why we need fallback at all) implicitly requires a fallback that can scale to primary access, yet the budget and 24-month timeline may not support true universal accessibility. None of the options adequately address what happens if the fallback cannot match the security assurance level (eIDAS 'Substantial' or higher) that MitID currently provides, creating a regulatory and political credibility gap.

name: Regulatory Approval and Certification Path
consequences: The project's demonstrators are explicitly non-production and non-certified, but phase three requires converting technical feasibility and demonstrator evidence into formal procurement and regulatory language. The path to that conversion is unclear: the project can pursue informal policy influence (shaping Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's AltID design guidance without formal certification), seek inclusion in official regulatory reviews or eIDAS consultations, or attempt to establish a formal certification or assurance framework for alternative authentication approaches. Each path has different timelines, institutional dependencies, and probability of surviving incumbent resistance. Regulatory approval is the highest-impact outcome but is also the slowest and most uncertain.
options: Pursue informal policy and procurement influence by positioning the demonstrators and feasibility report as evidence supporting platform-neutrality language in existing AltID governance documents and vendor RFP templates, working through sympathetic officials and consultants without requiring formal certification or regulatory review processes.

Initiate a formal standards or certification initiative for alternative authentication pathways under Danish or EU regulatory bodies, establishing an official track for validating platform-neutral approaches and creating a durable institutional foundation for future fallback-access deployments even if current AltID procurement does not immediately adopt them.

Engage with MitID governance structures directly to propose modification or supplementation of the MitID trust model to accommodate platform-neutral fallback access, framing it as service-continuity enhancement rather than disruption, and seeking inclusion of fallback access in the next version of MitID certification requirements.
review: The core tension is between speed and durability. Informal policy influence can achieve near-term procurement language changes but offers no institutional protection if political priorities shift; formal certification creates a lasting framework but requires 12–18 months of process, likely extending beyond the project timeline and delaying real procurement impact. Direct engagement with MitID operators is the most direct route to meaningful integration but is also the highest-risk: the MitID operator has organizational incentives to maintain the existing Apple/Google model and may view the project as a threat. None of the options adequately address the scenario in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen formally acknowledges the platform-dependency risk but concludes that maintaining single-operator control (MitID) is more important than achieving platform-neutrality, effectively shelving the fallback-access requirement in favor of incremental MitID hardening.

name: Incumbent Resistance and Operational Leverage
consequences: The project operates in an environment where MitID operators, platform vendors, and existing identity-ecosystem stakeholders have strong incentives to maintain the status quo. The project can attempt to minimize friction by framing its work as complementary rather than disruptive, seek pre-emptive alignment with key incumbents, or adopt a more assertive public-accountability and transparency approach that treats incumbent resistance as a legitimate governance issue. The first approach trades visibility and political pressure for smoother institutional relationships; the second accepts relationship friction but maximizes leverage to force formal consideration of the platform-neutrality issue. The choice affects whether the project is perceived as a partner or a challenger.
options: Maintain a collaborative, 'complementary-solutions' framing throughout all stakeholder engagement, positioning the demonstrators and fallback access as strengthening resilience and regulatory compliance without threatening existing MitID operations, and establishing regular technical working groups with MitID operators, platform vendors, and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to de-escalate tensions and incorporate their feedback.

Adopt a transparent accountability framing that formally documents MitID operator and platform vendor positions on platform-neutrality and fallback access, publishing those positions alongside the project's feasibility report and demonstrators, creating public record of any resistance or refusals and framing the issue as a governance and supply-chain risk that citizens and elected officials have a right to understand.

Seek conditional pre-commitment from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the MitID operator early in phase one that they will formally respond to the fallback-access proposal within a specified timeline, creating institutional accountability for serious evaluation even if the outcome is rejection, and anchoring the project's credibility to explicit decision gates rather than informal influence.
review: The tension is between collaboration and accountability. A purely collaborative approach risks being perceived as toothless if incumbents simply absorb engagement sessions without substantive commitment; an aggressive accountability approach may provoke institutional hostility and cause stakeholders to view the project as a threat rather than a solution partner. The critical weakness is the absence of a clear alternative if MitID operators and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen conclude that platform-neutrality is not a priority for AltID and decline to incorporate fallback-access language—none of the options specify whether the project pivots to EU-level pressure, escalates to elected officials, or accepts a phase-three outcome where procurement influence is not secured but the feasibility work and demonstrators are published for future use.

name: Financing Continuity and Phase Gating
consequences: The project is budgeted at 10.5 million DKK across three phases with explicit phase gates (phase two conditional on phase-one deliverables, implying phase three is conditional on phase-two outcomes). If financing conditions are not met or are withdrawn between phases, the project loses momentum and stakeholder credibility. The project can structure contingency and de-scoping plans into phase gates (e.g., phase three down-scopes to publication and EU engagement if AltID procurement access is not achieved), or it can assume that successful completion of each phase guarantees the next phase's funding, deferring difficult contingency conversations. The approach shapes both budget realism and stakeholder expectations about what outcomes are certain versus conditional.
options: Establish explicit contingency de-scoping at each phase gate, committing in writing that if critical phase-two or phase-three conditions (AltID access, formal institutional response, financing continuation) are not met, the project will transition to a defined reduced-scope outcome (publication, coalition consolidation, EU engagement) rather than attempting to execute full scope with partial resources or extended timeline.

Negotiate multi-phase financing commitments upfront that decouple phase funding from performance conditions, guaranteeing that phase two and three resources are secured regardless of interim outcomes, accepting higher initial financing burden in exchange for insulation from political or budget volatility between phases.

Maintain the existing conditional funding structure but establish a contingency fund (equivalent to 10 percent of total budget) held in reserve for critical phase-transition activities, creating a buffer to sustain stakeholder engagement and demonstrator continuity if phase gates are delayed without derailing the overall timeline or forcing out-of-pocket institutional costs.
review: The tension is between realism and ambition. Conditional phase gates are fiscally prudent and force honest evaluation of progress, but they create risk that partial success (e.g., a published feasibility report but weak AltID access) leads to project termination even though EU-level or future-procurement impact remains viable. Multi-phase financing security removes funding risk but requires attracting unusually confident institutional backing for a 24-month policy-influence project with uncertain outcomes. The deepest weakness is the absence of a clear metric for what constitutes success or failure at each gate—the current plan's success criteria are ambitious (formal written response, inclusion in official AltID documents) but do not specify what degree of incorporation counts as meaningful success or what counts as graceful de-scoping, leaving phase-gate decisions politically vulnerable.

name: Technical Demonstrator Ambition and Certification Pathway
consequences: The demonstrator scope directly signals credibility to regulators and procurement bodies, but higher ambition (moving toward eIDAS Level Substantial without production deployment) also increases cost, timeline risk, and the likelihood of certification delays. A lean, non-certified demonstrator is faster and cheaper but may be dismissed as a research toy; a more formally audited prototype builds institutional confidence but requires external security review, legal vetting, and coordination with relevant authorities that can stretch schedules by months.
options: Develop two minimal non-production demonstrators focused purely on architecture proof-of-concept, then publish the results without seeking external certification or formal security audit, accepting that adoption requires a separate production cycle.

Commission an independent external security assessment of the mobile demonstrator against NSIS Level Substantial criteria and publish the audit report prominently, adding three months and approximately 0.5 million DKK in cost but gaining formal third-party credibility.

Design the demonstrators explicitly as candidates for a future formal evaluation pathway (PreAudit or Fast-Track under eIDAS2), positioning the project as the upstream feasibility work that enables subsequent certification rather than attempting certification itself.
review: Demonstrator ambition directly influences whether institutional stakeholders treat the work as credible evidence or as academic research outside the procurement cycle. The core tension is that certification adds cost and delays but is almost required for adoption; however, all three options assume external auditors or future certifiers will have bandwidth and interest to review this work. None of the options account for the possibility that certification bodies may be backlogged, uninterested in non-production prototypes, or unwilling to formally evaluate a platform-neutral fallback pathway because it competes with incumbent operators' business models.

name: MitID Operator Engagement: Collaboration versus Circumvention
consequences: Direct engagement with the MitID operator (Nets or relevant authority) can surface technical constraints, legal dependencies, and operator concerns early, potentially securing endorsement or at least non-opposition. However, the operator has strong incentives to maintain the status quo and may use engagement to delay or co-opt the project. Circumventing the operator and targeting procurement and standards bodies instead avoids giving them veto power but risks them positioning the project as undermining existing infrastructure.
options: Invite the MitID operator into project governance as a technical advisor or steering-committee observer, share findings in real time, and seek their buy-in for fallback-pathway requirements that could eventually become formal procurement language.

Publish findings and demonstrators without prior operator consultation, engage only through formal policy channels (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID governance, parliamentary committees), and force the operator to respond reactively to public and regulatory pressure.

Negotiate a limited technical partnership in which the operator provides access to anonymized dependency data and design documentation relevant to the feasibility study, without committing to endorse platform-neutral alternatives or modify MitID itself.
review: The operator's incentives are fundamentally misaligned with the project's sovereign-resilience goal, because the status quo benefits them commercially and operationally. The core tension is that transparency and collaboration build trust but also hand the operator information and opportunity to obstruct; circumvention avoids operator veto but risks them framing the project as a threat to national security or service continuity. None of the options addresses the possibility that the operator may have contractual obligations to Apple or Google that prevent them from supporting a platform-neutral fallback, or that they may face legal liability if they endorse alternatives seen as undermining their existing certification or service agreements.

name: Procurement Language and Timing: AltID Integration versus Parallel Standards Track
consequences: Embedding platform-neutrality and fallback requirements directly into AltID procurement documentation maximizes near-term influence and locks in design constraints before architectural lock-in occurs. However, AltID governance is already underway, decision gates may be closing, and procurement bodies may resist late-stage changes to scope or technical requirements. A parallel EU standards and technical working-group strategy builds longer-term alignment and broader buy-in but risks being sidelined as a slow-moving research track while AltID hardens existing dependencies.
options: Target AltID procurement directly with formal amendment proposals before month 12, positioning platform-neutrality and fallback requirements as essential to eIDAS2 compliance and supply-chain resilience, and accept rejection if the governance window closes.

Develop a detailed technical specification for platform-neutral fallback authentication and submit it through Danish representation on relevant EU Digital Identity Wallet and NSIS working groups, betting that European-level standards convergence will eventually pressure AltID to conform.

Pursue both pathways in parallel: submit procurement input to AltID while simultaneously engaging EU working groups, but signal to AltID that Danish compliance will follow European requirements, framing Danish adoption as part of a broader EU interoperability mandate rather than a unilateral Danish sovereign demand.
review: The procurement window for AltID may already be partially closed, and European standards harmonization is slow and often aspirational rather than binding on national implementation. The core tension is that immediate AltID procurement influence is high-impact but low-probability because architectural decisions are already underway; EU standards work is higher-probability but effects lag by years. None of the options accounts for the possibility that AltID stakeholders may have already made formal commitments to specific vendors or technology stacks that make supplier-neutral requirements a renegotiation rather than a design choice, or that EU Digital Identity Wallet standards may themselves encode dependencies on Google and Apple (or other vendor oligopolies) at the protocol level, making European alignment an insufficient lever.

name: Coalition Composition and Public Advocacy Intensity
consequences: A broad coalition spanning civil-society organizations, academic researchers, privacy advocates, and parliamentary figures amplifies policy pressure and media attention, but dilutes message consistency, introduces conflicting priorities, and may trigger incumbent and government pushback framed as politicization of infrastructure. A narrower, technical coalition of security experts and procurement specialists builds credibility and focus but limits political pressure and may be ignored as a special-interest group if public and media attention remain low.
options: Assemble a coalition of civil-society organizations, privacy groups, digital-rights NGOs, and at least one parliamentary party with a digital sovereignty platform, launch a public campaign around resilience and supplier concentration risk, and accept that political framing may overshadow technical details.

Maintain a tight coalition of 3–5 recognized security researchers, Danish and EU digital-identity standards experts, and procurement officers, operate through formal channels (policy consultations, standards submissions, working groups), and communicate primarily through technical reports and regulatory testimony.

Build a two-tier coalition structure: a formal technical steering committee of recognized experts and institutional stakeholders, paired with a broader advocacy group of civil-society organizations that amplify findings to media and public audiences without speaking for the technical work itself.
review: Coalition strategy directly affects political leverage and media resonance, but broader coalitions introduce mission creep, inconsistent messaging, and risk that the project becomes perceived as part of a broader anti-tech or nationalist agenda rather than a focused infrastructure-resilience initiative. The core tension is that political pressure accelerates regulatory attention but may also harden incumbent and government resistance if the framing is seen as destabilizing or ideologically motivated. None of the options address how to maintain coalition cohesion if early momentum stalls, how to prevent civil-society partners from pursuing parallel campaigns that contradict the project's specific platform-neutrality and fallback focus, or how to insulate the technical work from political blame if external events (e.g., a US–EU tech dispute or a geopolitical incident) reshape the political context in which this work is interpreted.

name: Fallback Authentication Modality: Mobile-Native versus Browser-Plus-Hardware versus Hybrid
consequences: A mobile-native fallback (e.g., GrapheneOS with FIDO2) keeps the authentication experience familiar but still embeds dependency on Android-compatible infrastructure and device distribution. A browser-plus-hardware-token approach (e.g., FIDO2 security key) eliminates mobile-OS dependency but requires citizens to own and manage hardware tokens and accept a different authentication flow for critical services. A hybrid strategy supporting both modalities maximizes resilience but adds complexity, cost, and implementation burden across government service integrations.
options: Demonstrate a platform-neutral mobile fallback using open-source Android compatibility (GrapheneOS or equivalent) as the primary modality, arguing that reduced dependence on Google App Store controls and proprietary trust APIs counts as meaningful sovereignty even if Android itself remains part of the stack.

Design the demonstrator around hardware-token-based authentication (FIDO2 security keys, smartcards) as the primary platform-independent fallback, positioning browser-based access as the universal access path and accepting that adoption requires user education and token distribution infrastructure.

Implement both mobile-native (GrapheneOS) and hardware-token pathways in the demonstrators, with the explicit acknowledgment that full resilience requires supporting multiple modalities and that procurement requirements should mandate fallback-capable system design rather than specifying a single technical pathway.
review: The choice of fallback modality directly affects adoption friction and the project's claims about true platform independence. The core tension is that mobile-native fallbacks remain vulnerable to Android ecosystem dynamics and Google's influence over the broader Android ecosystem, while hardware-token approaches have dramatically lower adoption because they require physical token distribution and new user behavior. None of the options account for the reality that most Danish citizens do not carry hardware tokens and may resist authentication mechanisms perceived as inconvenient compared to fingerprint or face recognition on their existing phones. A hybrid approach reduces the project's ability to make a clean argument about which modality is sovereign, and government procurement bodies may use modality ambiguity as a reason to defer migration.

name: External Review and Institutional Vetting: Third-Party Validation versus Self-Published Authority
consequences: Commissioning independent security review, legal vetting, and institutional validation (e.g., from Danish Standards Foundation, a recognized EU digital-identity body, or international standards organization) adds legitimacy and shields the project from accusations of bias or incompetence. However, external reviewers operate on their own schedules, may impose conditions or caveats that weaken claims, and charge fees that consume budget with no guarantee of favorable outcomes. Publishing findings internally with academic peer review is faster and cheaper but leaves the project vulnerable to dismissal as self-serving or lacking rigorous external oversight.
options: Commission external security audit, legal review, and institutional endorsement from recognized Danish and EU bodies before publishing any major findings, treating external validation as a gating criterion for credibility and media release.

Publish the feasibility report and demonstrator findings under academic peer review and through established policy-research channels (Danish Institute for International Studies, RAND Europe, or equivalent), relying on peer review and institutional affiliation for credibility rather than seeking dedicated external certification.

Conduct internal technical review by the project team and steering committee, then submit findings to open critical commentary from the digital-identity research and standards community, framing transparency and reproducibility rather than external validation as the credibility mechanism.
review: External validation adds credibility but introduces scheduling risk, cost unpredictability, and potential for external reviewers to impose conditions (e.g., demanding further testing or caveats) that delay publication or weaken advocacy impact. The core tension is that institutional authority matters for procurement influence, but external bodies may have their own relationships with incumbent operators or institutional interests in maintaining status quo. None of the options account for the possibility that recognized external authorities may decline to endorse a platform-neutral fallback pathway because they view it as economically disruptive, beyond their mandate, or too politically controversial; or that internal peer review may be dismissed by skeptical institutional stakeholders as lack of independent validation, creating a circular credibility problem.

name: Geopolitical and EU-Institutional Timing: Proactive Standardization versus Reactive Response to External Events
consequences: Proactively engaging EU Digital Identity Wallet standardization processes and framing Danish work as upstream input builds long-term alignment and positions the project as driving rather than following European requirements. However, EU standardization is slow and often produces lowest-common-denominator outcomes that preserve incumbent flexibility. Timing the project to react to external geopolitical events (e.g., US trade restrictions, new EU digital-sovereignty directives, or transatlantic tech disputes) can amplify urgency and political will, but exposes the project to unpredictable external events and risks being swept into polarized geopolitical narratives that overshadow technical substance.
options: Embed the project as an active contributor to ongoing EU Digital Identity Wallet and NSIS working groups from month 6 onward, shape emerging standards toward platform-neutrality requirements, and accept that influence accumulates slowly and effects may lag by 24–36 months.

Monitor geopolitical developments and external digital-sovereignty initiatives (e.g., EU-level responses to US tech policy, emerging GAIA-X or digital-sovereignty frameworks, NATO digital resilience agendas) and time publication and advocacy to coincide with windows of heightened policy attention and receptivity to supplier-resilience arguments.

Pursue both pathways with explicit contingency planning: maintain steady EU standards engagement while simultaneously preparing accelerated advocacy and procurement outreach if external geopolitical events create urgency, positioning the project as ready to scale quickly if policy windows open.
review: Proactive EU engagement builds legitimacy and long-term alignment but is vulnerable to being sidelined or diluted as EU processes accommodate multiple member states with different concerns. Reactive timing amplifies near-term political leverage but makes the project hostage to unpredictable external events and risks tying the work to partisan or zero-sum geopolitical narratives that undermine technical credibility. The core tension is that meaningful influence on European standards requires multi-year engagement with limited visibility or near-term wins, while geopolitical windows are typically brief and may close as quickly as they open. None of the options address how to prevent the project's technical work from being weaponized in broader transatlantic tech disputes, how to maintain coalition focus if geopolitical framing overshadows the resilience and sovereignty arguments that sustain Danish buy-in, or how to recover strategic momentum if a geopolitical window opens but closes before the project has built sufficient technical or institutional credibility to capitalize on it.

name: Demonstrator Risk Tolerance and Production-Readiness Boundary
consequences: Deciding whether demonstrators remain fully isolated from live systems or attempt limited integration with sandbox environments determines both technical credibility and regulatory exposure. A tighter boundary (fully isolated) reduces approval friction but risks being dismissed as 'not real'; a looser boundary (sandbox access) strengthens validation but triggers security review, operator consent, and potential delays. The choice also shapes how phase-two findings influence phase-three procurement language.
options: Operate demonstrators entirely offline and against synthetic identity-provider environments, then publish comparative protocol analysis against live MitID architecture without requesting operator access or formal integration.

Request formal sandbox or limited-scope test access from the MitID operator and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, obtaining written agreement on scope, security review obligations, and non-disclosure boundaries before building.

Build demonstrators to run against synthetic environments in phase two, but prepare a detailed technical integration protocol for phase three that shows exactly how a fallback path could connect to real AltID infrastructure under controlled conditions without requiring operator pre-approval.
review: The core tension is that isolation guarantees speed and autonomy but invites dismissal as 'theoretical', while operator integration secures credibility but hands them veto power over scope and timeline. None of the options address the likelihood that the MitID operator will characterize sandbox requests as precedent-setting and demand formal liability agreements or security certifications before granting any access, effectively stalling phase-two work or forcing it underground.

name: Academic and Civil-Society Visibility versus Strategic Discretion
consequences: Publishing interim findings and demonstrator results early and publicly builds momentum, external accountability, and pressure on government bodies, but also alerts incumbent operators and EU platform providers to specific technical approaches, giving them time to preemptively harden defenses or lobby against procurement language. Holding publication until phase-three completion protects tactical surprise but risks losing coalition energy and media attention.
options: Publish the phase-one feasibility report and all technical findings from phase two immediately upon completion, with supplementary op-eds and conference presentations, to maximize public pressure and coalition visibility before phase-three procurement windows close.

Release only anonymized or high-level findings during phase two, embargo detailed technical demonstrator code and architecture until phase three, then coordinate a simultaneous publication push timed to coincide with formal AltID design reviews or government procurement consultations.

Establish a private technical advisory board comprising universities, privacy NGOs, and sympathetic Folketinget members who receive full interim findings under confidentiality, while publishing only generic resilience and supplier-risk messaging in public channels.
review: Early public visibility creates political cover and coalition momentum, but incumbent operators and Apple/Google will likely respond with lobbying, friendly media coverage emphasizing security and user experience costs, and formal warnings to Danish authorities about destabilizing the MitID trust model. The options assume civil-society pressure will outweigh commercial and institutional inertia, but do not account for how operators might frame platform-neutrality requirements as a threat to fraud prevention or data security, forcing government to choose between sovereignty and immediate security optics.

name: GrapheneOS Dependency and Fallback Platform Flexibility
consequences: Using GrapheneOS or a comparable privacy-respecting Android variant for the mobile demonstrator reduces platform vendor influence but introduces a new dependency risk: GrapheneOS is a small, volunteer-led project with limited resources and uncertain long-term sustainability. If the project's credibility rests on GrapheneOS as the fallback, changes to its roadmap, security model, or maintainer capacity directly threaten the value proposition. Alternatively, accepting standard Android (even with modifications) maintains broader ecosystem compatibility but compromises the sovereignty claim.
options: Commit to GrapheneOS as the primary platform, document its architecture and security model comprehensively, and explicitly acknowledge platform-switch risk as a dependency that requires Danish or EU institutional commitment to long-term sustainability and funding.

Build the mobile demonstrator on a standard Android codebase with privacy-hardening overlays that could run on multiple vendor-neutral Android distributions, prioritizing portability and vendor-independence over deep platform optimization.

Develop the mobile demonstrator as a framework-agnostic prototype using cross-platform abstractions, then instantiate it on both GrapheneOS and a second Android-compatible system (such as Lineage or a custom build), proving the authentication logic is not locked to a single platform.
review: Choosing a small-project like GrapheneOS addresses the underlying concern (reducing Apple/Google dependency) but replaces it with a sustainability and institutional-support risk that the project cannot resolve alone. Standard Android modifications avoid this but undermine the core argument that platform-vendor control is the problem. None of the options include a pathway for formal partnership or funding commitment with the chosen platform maintainers, leaving demonstrator credibility dependent on a project that may deprioritize identity use cases.

name: EU Institutional Alignment: Bilateral Standards Pressure versus Coordination with Broader Wallet Initiatives
consequences: Engaging the EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups and standards bodies (such as the EIDAS2 technical subcommittees) amplifies procurement pressure on Denmark and creates a multinational bulwark against incumbent pushback, but requires compromising on timeline and language to align with slower EU consensus processes. Bilateral engagement with specific member states accelerates influence but risks being isolated as a Danish peculiarity rather than a systemic EU concern.
options: Prioritize direct engagement with the EIDAS2 technical working groups and European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) committees on wallet architecture, positioning the platform-neutrality findings as input to official standards development rather than external advocacy.

Conduct bilateral outreach to other Nordic and EU member states facing similar platform-dependency issues (such as Germany, the Netherlands, or Sweden), building a coalition that tabled joint procurement and standards language at EU forums by phase three.

Engage both EU standards bodies and member-state coalitions in parallel, but maintain independent Danish procurement messaging; frame EU engagement as a longer-term normalization process while securing national AltID language as a near-term win.
review: EU institutional process is slow and consensus-driven; by the time standards language is finalized, AltID procurement may already be locked. Bilateral state coalitions are faster but risk being dismissed as a subset concern. None of the options include a realistic assessment of EU-level veto power: Apple and Google are not members, but their industry associations and legal teams are active in standards discussions, and they will argue that platform-neutral requirements undermine fraud prevention and app security, potentially blocking language the project favors.

name: Contingency Access Model: Technical Completeness versus Regulatory Pragmatism
consequences: Defining what 'fallback' authentication actually means—whether it must work for all citizen categories (healthcare, tax, banking, emergency services), or whether it can be scoped to a subset (e.g., non-financial access only)—determines both demonstrator scope and the credibility of the resilience claim. Comprehensive fallback is more convincing but requires much larger demonstrator effort and triggers more regulatory scrutiny. Limited-scope fallback is faster to demonstrate but invites dismissal as insufficient.
options: Design the fallback path to cover the same transaction types and citizen categories as the current MitID access model, requiring the demonstrator to authenticate against public-sector services across healthcare, tax, banking, and emergency portals, accepting larger phase-two scope and formal security review.

Scope the fallback demonstrator to non-financial public-administration and healthcare access (tax filing, license renewal, appointment booking), explicitly excluding banking and emergency services as phase-three extension work, and frame this as a staged resilience roadmap.

Develop a hybrid model where fallback authentication is full-scope but contingent on emergency-declaration activation (i.e., fallback APIs and protocols are available on all platforms, but normal operation uses MitID, and fallback only activates if the primary platform becomes unavailable), reducing demonstrator complexity while maintaining comprehensive backup credentials.
review: Comprehensive fallback is technically sound and politically compelling, but it requires security certifications and regulatory approval that phase-two timeline does not support; institutions may demand that fallback be equivalent to MitID in fraud detection and liability, making certification impossible without years of operational history. Limited scope avoids this but weakens the sovereignty argument. The hybrid emergency-activation model is clever but assumes a technical and legal framework for 'fallback activation' that does not yet exist; authorities will demand specification of who decides when fallback is triggered, creating a new governance dependency.

name: Incumbent Engagement Posture: Collaborative Integration versus Constructive Critique
consequences: Framing the project as supportive of MitID resilience (and positioning fallback access as complementary security, not competing replacement) gains operator goodwill and information access but risks neutering procurement language and allowing the operator to co-opt the language while changing nothing. Explicitly positioning the project as a critique of MitID platform dependency is more honest but guarantees operator hostility and legal pressure.
options: Maintain formal collaborative dialogue with the MitID operator throughout all phases, positioning fallback access as a Danish resilience requirement they can voluntarily adopt, and frame procurement pressure as external regulatory compliance rather than project critique.

Establish the project as an independent technical and policy critique of current MitID architecture, engage the operator only for factual clarifications under documented conditions, and direct procurement messaging to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and political stakeholders rather than through operator cooperation.

Build relationships with enlightened operators and sympathetic technical staff within the MitID organization separately from official channels, leveraging informal influence to shape operator thinking on fallback models while maintaining public critique of platform lock-in and downstream pressuring institutional decision-makers.
review: Collaboration risks co-optation (the operator agrees to study fallback models indefinitely without commitment); critique risks legal harassment and exclusion from information access that the operator controls. The informal pathway assumes that technical staff and management can be separated—a fragile assumption if the operator perceives the project as threatening revenue or institutional autonomy. None of the options account for the operator's strong financial incentive to maintain the Apple/Google duopoly, since platform dependency reduces their own liability for authentication failures.

name: Financial Gating and Phase-Transition Criteria Flexibility
consequences: Strict phase-gating (phase-two funding released only if phase-one produces specific institutional engagement, published reports, and financing plans) ensures quality discipline and risk mitigation but introduces schedule risk if institutional traction is slower than expected or requires relationship-building that extends beyond month eight. Flexible gating allows work continuity but obscures whether earlier phases actually delivered decision-relevant outputs.
options: Implement hard phase gates as specified in the project plan: phase-two funding is released only upon published feasibility report, documented institutional engagement with at least one major stakeholder, and a complete financing plan for the remainder of the project, with no exceptions or partial releases.

Establish tiered funding releases within phase two (30% upon feasibility report publication, 40% upon first institutional engagement, 30% upon financing plan completion) to allow demonstrator work to begin earlier while maintaining risk checks.

Define phase gates around technical milestones (demonstrator architecture review, security audit checkpoint) rather than institutional engagement, recognizing that government responsiveness is uncertain, and reserve institutional-leverage milestones for phase-three success criteria.
review: Strict gating ensures accountability but may exhaust political capital before phase two even begins if institutional response is slow (Danish processes can take months). Tiered release reduces this risk but creates budget-tracking complexity and weakens the leverage point if early releases are interpreted as unconditional support. Technical gates decouple the project from institutional reality; demonstrators can be perfect and still miss the procurement window if AltID timelines shift or political priorities change. None of the options include contingency planning for the scenario where Digitaliseringsstyrelsen deprioritizes digital sovereignty in 2027 due to fiscal constraints or political shifts, forcing the project to choose between completing technically strong work or pursuing relevance it can no longer guarantee.
